candidates.title: Fiscal Year 2024 & 2025 Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities (BRIC)
candidates.rationale: BRIC funds infrastructure resilience projects that could involve construction contractors, but grants go to state/local governments — the applicant could potentially participate as a subcontractor, not a direct grantee.

candidates.title: FY 2025 EDA Public Works and Economic Adjustment Assistance Programs
candidates.rationale: EDA Public Works grants fund infrastructure construction in distressed areas and often involve for-profit contractors as subrecipients, but direct awards go to government entities or nonprofits, not for-profit firms.

candidates.title: Military-Connected Schools Construction, Modernization and Facilities Maintenance Program
candidates.rationale: Military-connected school construction is thematically relevant to a commercial construction firm, but grants are awarded to DoD-affiliated entities, not private contractors who would be hired separately via procurement.

candidates.title: State Veterans Home Construction Grant Program
candidates.rationale: State Veterans Home construction grants go to state governments to build facilities, not to private construction firms, though the applicant might compete for resulting construction contracts.

candidates.agencyName: U.S. Dept. of Treasury RESTORE Act Program
candidates.rationale: RESTORE Act construction grants are limited to Gulf Coast states (AL, FL, LA, MS, TX), making Arizona-based applicants geographically ineligible.

steps.message: Drafting for DHS-25-MT-047-00-98 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: A Phoenix-based for-profit general contractor (NAICS 236220, 12 employees, $2.4M revenue, 6 years operating) is seeking to apply to DHS opportunity DHS-25-MT-047-00-98. Because the opportunity description, eligibility text, funding range, award count, and close date are entirely absent, the skeleton below is built on DHS Mitigation program conventions and the applicant's commercial infrastructure profile — but must be verified against the actual solicitation before use.

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the specific infrastructure vulnerability or mitigation gap that this project will address. DHS mitigation grants typically require applicants to anchor need in documented threat/hazard data, community risk assessments, or FEMA Hazard Mitigation Plans. For a commercial contractor, this section must connect the firm's work to a public or community benefit — not purely private commercial gain.
promptsForApplicant: What specific natural hazard, physical threat, or infrastructure vulnerability in the Phoenix metro area does this project address — and what documented source (e.g., Arizona State Hazard Mitigation Plan, local HMP, DHS THIRA) quantifies that risk?
Who are the end beneficiaries of the mitigation work (e.g., a municipality, tribal nation, school district, utility)? Is there a signed MOU or letter of support from that entity?
What is the consequence of NOT completing this project — in terms of lives, property value, or critical infrastructure continuity?
Has the need been previously identified in any official planning document? If so, what is the document name, year, and page/section reference?

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe the technical scope of work, methodology, timeline, and deliverables. DHS mitigation programs expect cost-effective, proven construction or engineering methods aligned with FEMA's Hazard Mitigation Assistance guidance. The approach must demonstrate that activities are construction/infrastructure-focused and not planning-only or research activities.
promptsForApplicant: What are the specific construction or infrastructure activities your firm will perform (e.g., flood-proofing, seismic retrofitting, hardening of critical facilities)?
What is the project timeline broken into phases, with milestone dates? What is your realistic start date assuming a notice-to-proceed?
What design standards, building codes, or DHS/FEMA technical specifications will govern the work?
Who are your subcontractors or technical partners, and what portions of work will they perform? Do any subcontractors hold relevant certifications or set-aside statuses?
How does the project scope fit within your firm's current capacity — given 12 employees and $2.4M annual revenue — without compromising existing contracts?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that the applicant has the technical expertise, financial stability, management systems, and past performance to execute a federally funded mitigation project. DHS will scrutinize whether a for-profit firm with no noted socioeconomic certifications has a legitimate program role versus acting as a pass-through.
promptsForApplicant: What are 3 comparable completed projects (scope, dollar value, client type) that demonstrate relevant mitigation or infrastructure hardening experience?
Does the firm have an active SAM.gov registration with a valid Unique Entity Identifier (UEI)? What is the expiration date?
Does the firm have an established accounting system capable of tracking federal funds separately and producing reports compliant with 2 CFR Part 200?
Who is the designated Project Manager for this award, and what are their credentials (license, certifications, years of relevant experience)?
Has your firm ever received federal funding before? If yes, were there any audit findings, disallowed costs, or performance issues?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Itemize all costs by category and justify each line against the project scope. DHS mitigation programs require cost-effectiveness analysis and, for construction projects, typically require a benefit-cost analysis (BCA) demonstrating that project benefits exceed costs. For-profit applicants should expect scrutiny of profit margins and indirect cost rates.
promptsForApplicant: What is the total project cost, and how is it broken down by category: direct labor, subcontracts, materials/equipment, travel, indirect costs, and profit?
What is your indirect cost rate — is it a federally negotiated rate, a de minimis 10% MTDC rate per 2 CFR §200.414, or a proposed rate? Provide documentation.
Have you prepared or can you prepare a FEMA Benefit-Cost Analysis (BCA) using the FEMA BCA Toolkit? What is the projected benefit-cost ratio?
Is there a cost-share or match requirement for this opportunity? If so, what is the source and percentage of non-federal match your firm or a partner will provide?
How were labor rates determined — prevailing wage (Davis-Bacon), market rate, or another basis? Are Davis-Bacon Act requirements anticipated for this project?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define measurable outputs and outcomes tied to DHS/FEMA mitigation program goals. The agency will want to see how success is defined, tracked, and reported over the period of performance.
promptsForApplicant: What are the 2-3 primary quantifiable outcomes of this project (e.g., number of structures hardened, estimated dollar value of property protected, number of people whose risk is reduced)?
What data will you collect during and after construction to verify that mitigation goals were achieved?
Who is responsible for post-construction monitoring or reporting, and for how long after project completion?
How will the completed infrastructure be maintained, and by whom, to ensure long-term effectiveness of the mitigation investment?

watchOuts: CRITICAL: The solicitation number DHS-25-MT-047-00-98 returned no description, eligibility text, funding range, award count, or close date. You must locate and read the full Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO) on Grants.gov or SAM.gov before proceeding — every section of this skeleton is based on DHS mitigation program conventions and could be wrong for the actual solicitation.

For-profit eligibility: Many DHS Hazard Mitigation grant programs (e.g., BRIC, HMGP) restrict direct applicants to state/local governments, tribes, or territories. A for-profit contractor may only be eligible as a subrecipient or contractor under a government prime — confirm whether your firm can apply directly or must partner with an eligible government entity.

No socioeconomic certifications: This firm holds no WOSB, SDVOSB, 8(a), HUBZone, or SDB designations. If the solicitation includes set-aside components or scoring preferences for disadvantaged businesses, the firm will not receive those points — factor this into competitive strategy.
SAM.gov registration must be active and not expired at time of application and throughout the award. Lapses are an automatic administrative disqualifier.

NAICS 236220 (Commercial and Institutional Building Construction) must match the NAICS code designated in the solicitation. If DHS has assigned a different NAICS, the application may be deemed non-responsive.

A 6-year-old firm with $2.4M revenue may face financial capacity scrutiny for large federal awards. Be prepared to provide audited financials, a line of credit letter, or bonding capacity documentation.
Davis-Bacon Act wage requirements almost certainly apply to any federally funded construction project. Failure to account for prevailing wages in the budget is a common fatal cost error.
If a Benefit-Cost Analysis is required and the BCR is below 1.0, the project is ineligible regardless of other merits. Do not submit without running the FEMA BCA Toolkit first.

Arizona is a non-attainment area for certain EPA standards; environmental compliance reviews (NEPA, Section 106) may be required and can significantly extend timelines — do not underestimate schedule.

result.queryRationale: Covers nonprofit workforce development and trade skills programs; 'rural' targets USDA and EDA rural opportunity notices relevant to Vermont geography without over-specifying location.

candidates.title: Agriculture and Food Research Initiative Competitive Grants Program Education and Workforce Development
candidates.agencyName: National Institute of Food and Agriculture
candidates.rationale: Education and workforce development focus aligns well with the applicant's trade-skills training mission, and USDA/NIFA programs are accessible to small nonprofits in rural states like Vermont, though the agricultural framing may not perfectly match general trade-skills training.

candidates.title: Rural Communities Opioid Response Program (RCORP)-Planning
candidates.rationale: Rural Vermont location is a plus, but RCORP-Planning targets opioid response coalitions rather than workforce training nonprofits, making this a weak thematic fit.

candidates.title: Rural Communities Opioid Response Program (RCORP)-Impact
candidates.rationale: Same rural eligibility advantage as RCORP-Planning but the opioid-response implementation focus is misaligned with trade-skills workforce training.

candidates.title: U.S.-Argentina English for the Energy Sector & Minerals Workforce Development
candidates.rationale: Workforce development keyword match, but this is a bilateral U.S.-Argentina English-language energy-sector program requiring international capacity that a 3-person Vermont nonprofit almost certainly lacks.

candidates.title: National Defense Education Program (NDEP) Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) Consortia Request for Information (RFI) for the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense (Research & Engineering)
candidates.rationale: STEM and workforce themes are tangentially related, but this DoD RFI targets large research/defense consortia, not small rural nonprofit training providers.

steps.message: Drafting for HRSA-26-036 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: A 4-year-old Vermont nonprofit (NAICS 611430) providing trade-skills training to under-employed adults is applying to HRSA-26-036, a Health Resources & Services Administration opportunity whose full description and eligibility criteria are not yet available. Because HRSA primarily funds health workforce, rural health, and community health programs, the applicant's angle should connect trade-skills training to health workforce pipeline development or social determinants of health outcomes for under-employed adults in rural/central Vermont. Critical caution: the applicant must first confirm that this opportunity is open to non-health-sector training nonprofits before investing further effort.

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the problem this project addresses within the context of HRSA's mission areas (health workforce shortages, rural health access, underserved populations). Connect under-employment in central Vermont to health outcomes and workforce gaps. Use local, state, and national data to quantify need.
promptsForApplicant: What specific health-related workforce shortages exist in central Vermont that your trade-skills training could help address (e.g., health facility maintenance, medical equipment technicians, construction of rural health infrastructure)?
What data — county-level, state WIOA reports, Vermont Department of Labor statistics — quantify the under-employment rate and demographic profile of your target population in the 05641 service area?
How does under-employment in your target population create or worsen health disparities (e.g., lack of insurance, food insecurity, housing instability) in central Vermont?
What existing services or programs in your region are NOT meeting this need, and what gap does your organization fill that others cannot?
Can you demonstrate that central Vermont qualifies as a rural or health professional shortage area (HPSA) or medically underserved area (MUA/MUP) under HRSA definitions?

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe the specific activities, timeline, and methods the project will use. Because the full FOA description is unavailable, structure this section to be adaptable but ensure it clearly ties training methodology to measurable health-workforce or health-outcome goals consistent with HRSA priorities.
promptsForApplicant: What specific trades or skills will be taught during the grant period, and how do those skills connect explicitly to health sector facilities, services, or workforce needs?
What is your current training curriculum and how will it be modified or expanded if this grant is awarded?
What is the projected timeline of activities (recruitment, training cohorts, credentialing, job placement) across a 12-month and/or multi-year performance period?
What partners — employers, community health centers, hospitals, technical colleges — have committed to this project, and what is their specific role (MOUs, letters of support)?
How will you recruit participants who are disadvantaged or under-employed, and what wraparound supports (transportation, childcare, stipends) will you provide to reduce attrition?
What evidence base or model (e.g., sector-based training, earn-and-learn, incumbent worker training) informs your approach, and is there peer-reviewed or programmatic evidence supporting it?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that a 3-person, 4-year-old nonprofit with $180K annual revenue has the infrastructure, fiscal controls, and track record to manage a federal award. Reviewers will scrutinize organizational capacity heavily given the small staff size.
promptsForApplicant: What federal or state grants has your organization previously managed, and what were the award amounts, outcomes, and compliance records?
With only 3 employees, who specifically will serve as Project Director and Financial Officer, and what are their qualifications in grant management and workforce training?
Do you have a federally negotiated indirect cost rate agreement (NICRA), or will you use the 10% de minimis rate? Have you registered in SAM.gov with an active UEI?
What financial controls, accounting systems (e.g., QuickBooks, fund accounting software), and audit history demonstrate your ability to manage federal funds?
What subcontractors or consultants will you engage to supplement your 3-person staff, and how will you ensure their compliance with federal requirements?
Has your organization undergone a Single Audit (2 CFR Part 200 Subpart F), and if not, do you have audited financial statements for the past two fiscal years?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Justify every line item with specificity and tie costs to project activities. For a small nonprofit, reviewers will look for reasonableness of salaries, appropriate indirect cost treatment, and absence of unallowable costs under HHS/HRSA cost principles (2 CFR Part 200).
promptsForApplicant: What percentage of each staff member's time will be charged to this grant, and how does that align with their described project roles?
What equipment or supplies are required, and can you justify each as directly necessary for the project rather than general organizational operations?
Are any costs for construction, renovation, or capital equipment included? If so, what HRSA-specific prior approval requirements apply?
How did you calculate fringe benefit rates, and are they consistent with your organization's established written policies?
What travel is budgeted, and does it comply with the Federal Travel Regulation (FTR) or HHS travel guidelines?
If you plan to request participant stipends or support costs, how have you confirmed these are allowable under this specific HRSA program?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define measurable, time-bound performance measures aligned with HRSA's standard performance reporting requirements. HRSA typically requires both process and outcome measures tied to health workforce or population health goals.
promptsForApplicant: What are your numeric targets for the grant period: number of participants enrolled, completing training, earning credentials, and placed in employment?
How will you define and measure 'success' for participants in terms of wage gain, employment retention at 6 and 12 months, and health coverage access?
What data collection tools, systems, and staff responsibilities will track participant progress and generate reports for HRSA's Electronic Handbooks (EHB) or other reporting portals?
Who will conduct your evaluation — internal staff or an independent evaluator — and what is their methodology (pre/post, comparison group, case study)?
How will you use evaluation findings during the project period to make mid-course corrections (continuous quality improvement)?
What is your sustainability plan if HRSA funding ends — how will these training programs and outcomes continue beyond the grant period?

watchOuts: HRSA-26-036 has no published description or eligibility text — the applicant MUST retrieve the full Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO) from Grants.gov using this opportunity number before assuming eligibility, as HRSA predominantly funds health sector organizations and this trade-skills nonprofit may not qualify.

HRSA frequently restricts awards to specific entity types (e.g., Federally Qualified Health Centers, State/local governments, academic health centers) — confirm that 501(c)(3) nonprofits without a health services designation are explicitly listed as eligible applicants.
With only $180K annual revenue, the organization may fall below an unstated minimum financial capacity threshold; some HRSA NOFOs require demonstrated ability to manage awards of a certain size.
SAM.gov registration must be active with a current UEI at time of application submission — verify now, as renewals can take 1-4 weeks.

A 4-year operating history is short; if the NOFO requires a minimum number of years in operation (commonly 2-5 years depending on program), confirm the count-from date matches your IRS determination letter date, not your incorporation date.

If the funding amount is large enough to trigger Single Audit requirements (expenditures exceeding $750,000 in a federal fiscal year), ensure your accounting infrastructure can support audit readiness.

The 'disadvantaged' ownership designation under SBA standards applies to for-profit firms; for nonprofits, confirm whether HRSA's program uses a different definition of 'disadvantaged' and whether this designation provides any scoring preference.

Vermont's rural and HPSA designations must be verified against current HRSA data tools (HRSA Data Warehouse / Find-a-HPSA) — designations change annually and outdated citations can undermine the Statement of Need.

Because the close date is unknown, monitor Grants.gov and HRSA's website daily for the published NOFO; federal close dates can be as short as 30 days from publication, leaving insufficient time to build partnerships and gather letters of support at the last minute.

Lobbying costs and certain advocacy activities are unallowable under federal awards (2 CFR 200.450) — ensure no portion of the budget reflects these activities even if they are part of the organization's normal operations.

candidates.rationale: CyberAICorps is a scholarship-for-service program for students and higher-education institutions, not a grant available to a for-profit consultancy.

steps.message: Drafting for 25-531 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: Without a grant description, eligibility text, funding range, or agency identity, no opportunity-specific structure can be responsibly derived. The skeleton below applies the default section framework and flags the critical information gaps that must be resolved before a compliant application can be drafted for a for-profit, woman-owned cybersecurity firm (NAICS 541512) in Austin, TX with 3 years of operation.

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the problem or gap this project addresses. Because the grant description is absent, the applicant must infer the funding priority from the opportunity number (25-531) and agency. Until the actual notice is located, draft this section around the documented cybersecurity risk landscape for mid-market Texas businesses, positioning the firm's retainer and assessment model as a direct response.
promptsForApplicant: What specific cybersecurity threat or service gap in your target market does this project address, and what quantitative evidence (incident rates, unmet demand data, regional statistics) supports that gap?
Why is a for-profit, woman-owned small business with a retainer-based model better positioned than alternatives (larger contractors, nonprofits, government IT shops) to solve this problem?

Have you retrieved the official Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO) or Solicitation for opportunity 25-531 from SAM.gov, Grants.gov, or the issuing agency portal? If not, do so immediately before drafting further.

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe the methodology, activities, timeline, and deliverables. Without the NOFO, scope must be held as a placeholder. Structure activities around your core service lines (assessments, incident-response retainers) and any proposed expansion or innovation the grant would fund.
promptsForApplicant: What specific activities will grant funds pay for — new service capabilities, staff hires, tooling, geographic expansion, or something else?
What is your proposed project period, and what are the major milestones and deliverables at each phase?
How does your incident-response retainer model translate into a grant-funded deliverable rather than a commercial contract? What is the public or programmatic benefit?
Which frameworks or standards (NIST CSF, CIS Controls, CMMC, etc.) govern your methodology, and how will you document compliance with them?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that the firm has the technical expertise, past performance, and administrative capacity to execute the project. With only 8 employees and 3 years of operation, the narrative must be especially concrete and evidence-based.
promptsForApplicant: What relevant certifications does your team hold (CISSP, CEH, CISA, OSCP, etc.) and what percentage of staff hold them?
Describe up to three past performance examples — client type, scope, dollar value, and measurable outcome — that are analogous to the proposed project.
How will you manage project administration, reporting, and financial compliance with only 8 employees while maintaining client retainer obligations?
Does your firm currently have or is it able to obtain a Unique Entity Identifier (UEI) and active SAM.gov registration, which is required for virtually all federal awards?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Provide a cost-by-cost justification tied to project activities. Because funding range is unknown, build a modular budget that can be scaled. Ensure all costs are allowable, allocable, and reasonable under 2 CFR Part 200 if this is a federal grant, or the applicable FAR provisions if it is a contract.
promptsForApplicant: What are the specific budget line items (personnel, fringe, travel, equipment, supplies, contractual, indirect) and what activity does each line directly support?
What is your firm's current indirect cost rate, and do you have a negotiated rate agreement with a federal cognizant agency, or will you use the de minimis 10% MTDC rate?
Are any costs on this project shared with your commercial client retainers? If so, how will you document and separate allowable federal costs?
If the funding range is confirmed, does your total budget request fall within it, and have you accounted for cost-share requirements if any exist in the NOFO?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define measurable outputs and outcomes and explain how you will track and report them. Federal reviewers expect a logic model or equivalent linking inputs → activities → outputs → outcomes → impact.
promptsForApplicant: What are the specific, quantifiable outputs (e.g., number of assessments completed, number of organizations served, number of vulnerabilities remediated) expected by project end?
What short-term and long-term outcomes will you measure, and what data collection methods will you use (pre/post surveys, incident logs, client attestations)?
Who internally is responsible for evaluation, and do you have the data infrastructure to capture and report metrics on the grant's required schedule?
How will you sustain project outcomes or services after grant funding ends?

watchOuts: CRITICAL — No NOFO retrieved: Opportunity '25-531' cannot be validated without the issuing agency and official notice. Do not invest further drafting effort until you locate and read the full solicitation; eligibility, submission format, and review criteria are entirely unknown.

For-profit eligibility: Many federal grant programs (especially on Grants.gov under HHS, ED, or NSF) restrict awards to nonprofits, educational institutions, or government entities. Confirm that for-profit entities are explicitly eligible before proceeding.

Years in operation: At 3 years old, the firm may fall below minimum operating-history thresholds common in SBA programs, DHS cybersecurity grants, or NIST cooperative agreements. Verify no floor exists in the NOFO.

SAM.gov registration: Active registration with a valid UEI is a hard prerequisite for nearly all federal awards. If registration is lapsed or never initiated, begin immediately — processing can take 7–10 business days.
NAICS code alignment: NAICS 541512 (Computer Systems Design) must match the NAICS code(s) listed as eligible in the solicitation. A mismatch can disqualify the application at intake.

Small business size standard: For NAICS 541512, the SBA size standard is $34M average annual receipts. At $1.2M the firm qualifies as small, but confirm the NOFO does not impose a more restrictive revenue or employee ceiling.

Woman-owned certification: If the opportunity offers evaluation preference or set-aside status for WOSBs, ensure the firm holds or immediately pursues SBA WOSB certification — self-certification alone may be insufficient for some programs.

Indirect cost rate: Without a negotiated indirect cost rate agreement, the firm is limited to the 10% de minimis rate under 2 CFR 200.414(f). Using a higher rate without authorization is an audit finding risk.

Conflict of interest / commercial work separation: Grant-funded cybersecurity work delivered to the same clients on commercial retainers creates cost-allocation and conflict-of-interest risks that must be explicitly managed and disclosed.

summary.intent: My woman-owned cybersecurity consultancy in Texas has 8 employees and $1.2M revenue. Any SBA innovation grants?

shortlist.title: Cybersecurity Innovation for Cyberinfrastructure
blockers: No UEI/SAM.gov registration found in profile. Most federal grants require an active SAM.gov registration with a valid UEI as a baseline eligibility requirement. This is a likely hard blocker pending verification.

shortlist.title: Regional Alliances and Multistakeholder Partnerships to Stimulate (RAMPS) Cybersecurity Education and Workforce Development
blockers: No SAM.gov UEI is present in the applicant profile. Active SAM.gov registration with a valid UEI is a universal federal grant prerequisite (2 CFR §25.200). Without it, the applicant cannot receive a federal award regardless of other eligibility factors.

shortlist.title: Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) program, Phase I
Small Business Innovation Research Program (SBIR) Phase II

result.queryRationale: Targets the core NAICS 332710 activity (precision machining) and sector (manufacturing) using terms program officers write in NOFO titles; veteran ownership and Ohio eligibility are screened at application, not keyword, stage.

candidates.title: Precision Measurement Grant Program
candidates.agencyName: National Institute of Standards and Technology
candidates.rationale: Precision measurement is thematically adjacent to precision machining, but this NIST program typically targets research institutions and metrology laboratories rather than for-profit manufacturing startups.

candidates.title: Precision Approaches in Radiation Synthetic Combinations (PAIRS, RP1 Clinical Trial Optional)
candidates.rationale: Biomedical/radiation clinical research grant with no relevance to manufacturing, machining, or veteran-owned small businesses.

candidates.title: Enhancing Mechanistic Research on Precision Probiotic Therapies (R33 Clinical Trial Optional)
candidates.rationale: Probiotic therapy mechanistic research grant targeting biomedical researchers, entirely unrelated to precision machining or manufacturing.

candidates.title: Enhancing Mechanistic Research on Precision Probiotic Therapies (R61/R33 Clinical Trial Optional)
candidates.rationale: Same probiotic therapy R61/R33 program as PAR-25-210, no connection to applicant's manufacturing profile.

candidates.title: Neuromodulation/Neurostimulation Device Development for Mental Health Applications (R21 Clinical Trial Not Allowed)
candidates.rationale: Neuromodulation device development for mental health, a biomedical R&D grant incompatible with a precision machining manufacturer.

Applicant type restrictions, funding ranges, eligible NAICS codes, geographic restrictions, and ownership/size requirements are all unspecified in the opportunity record.
blockers: No SAM.gov UEI is present in the applicant profile. Active SAM.gov registration with a valid UEI is a universal prerequisite for federal grants; without it the applicant is currently ineligible to receive a federal award regardless of other factors.

result.notes: Critical next steps: (1) Register or verify active registration in SAM.gov and obtain a UEI before any application is submitted. (2) Retrieve and review the full official opportunity posting (FOA/NOFO) for 2026-NIST-PMGP-01 directly from grants.gov or NIST to obtain actual eligibility requirements. (3) Once eligibility text is available, re-evaluate against applicant attributes such as entity type (for-profit), NAICS 332710, 5 employees, ~$420K revenue, 1.5 years in operation, veteran-owned status, and Ohio location — all of which may be relevant depending on the actual program requirements. The PMGP designation may suggest a NIST-affiliated manufacturing or precision machining program, but this is speculative and cannot substitute for reviewing the actual FOA.

steps.message: Drafting for 2026-NIST-PMGP-01 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: A veteran-owned precision machining startup (NAICS 332710) in northeast Ohio with 1.5 years of operation and $420K annual revenue seeks NIST Manufacturing Extension Partnership or similar PMGP-designated federal manufacturing support funding. The proposal should foreground veteran ownership, regional aerospace/defense supply chain integration, and a clear growth trajectory despite early-stage status. Given the absence of grant-specific details, the skeleton below is structured around standard NIST PMGP conventions and flagged heavily for applicant verification.

sections.heading: Executive Summary
sections.guidance: Provide a concise snapshot of who you are, what you are requesting, and what outcome the investment will produce. For NIST-adjacent manufacturing grants, reviewers expect a direct tie between the firm's capabilities and national manufacturing competitiveness or supply chain resilience goals.
promptsForApplicant: What specific dollar amount are you requesting, and what will it be used to purchase or accomplish?
In one or two sentences, how does your precision machining capacity directly support U.S. aerospace or defense supply chain resilience?
What is the single most measurable outcome (jobs created, revenue growth, new contracts) you can credibly commit to if funded?

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the market or operational problem your business faces that this grant will address. NIST-framed grants typically require applicants to connect firm-level need to a broader manufacturing ecosystem gap — not just business growth for its own sake.
promptsForApplicant: What specific capability gap (equipment, workforce, certification, capacity) currently limits your ability to win or fulfill aerospace/defense contracts?
What evidence — quotes, lost bids, lead-time data — demonstrates that this gap is real and measurable today?
How does the northeast Ohio regional manufacturing ecosystem suffer if small precision machining suppliers like yours cannot scale?
Are there specific prime contractors or Tier 1 suppliers in your region you are unable to serve due to this gap?

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe exactly what activities the grant funds will support, in what sequence, and on what timeline. NIST manufacturing programs expect specificity: equipment model numbers, training providers, certification bodies, milestones, and responsible parties.
promptsForApplicant: What specific equipment, software, or services will you procure with grant funds — include make/model and vendor quotes if available?
What is your 12-to-24-month implementation timeline, broken into discrete phases with milestones?
Who internally or externally will manage project execution, and what is their relevant experience?
If equipment is involved, where will it be sited, and do you have the facility capacity (power, floor space, environmental) to install it?
What workforce training will accompany any capital investment, and who will provide it?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that your firm can execute the project and manage federal funds responsibly. Reviewers will scrutinize a 1.5-year-old, 5-person firm heavily on this section — you must proactively address early-stage risk with evidence of competent management and existing customer traction.
promptsForApplicant: What contracts or purchase orders are currently active or pending that demonstrate customer demand for your expanded capacity?
What is the background and relevant industry experience of the owner(s) and key personnel — specifically in precision machining, aerospace/defense, or small business management?
Does the firm have an active SAM.gov registration and all required federal contractor prerequisites in place?
What accounting system do you use, and has it ever been audited or reviewed externally?
Can you provide references from customers, suppliers, or industry partners who can attest to your technical quality?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Justify every line item as allowable, allocable, and reasonable. Federal cost principles (2 CFR 200 for grants, or equivalent for cooperative agreements) require that for-profit recipients demonstrate that no cost is duplicative of other federal funding and that cost-share, if required, is documented.
promptsForApplicant: What is the total project cost, and how much are you requesting versus contributing from non-federal sources?
Is any cost-share or matching requirement stated in the NOFO — and if so, what is your source of match (revenue, loan, owner equity)?
Are any of the proposed costs already covered by another federal contract, grant, or SBA program — and if so, how will you ensure no double-dipping?
What quotes or price comparisons support the reasonableness of each major cost line?
How will you track and report expenditures to the agency during the period of performance?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define success metrics that are specific, time-bound, and plausibly attributable to the grant investment. NIST-aligned programs typically expect job creation/retention numbers, revenue growth, productivity improvements, or supply chain impact — expressed as committed targets, not aspirations.
promptsForApplicant: How many net new full-time jobs will you create within 24 months of award, and what wage range will they carry?
What is your projected revenue increase attributable to this investment, expressed in dollars and percentage growth?
What new contracts, certifications (e.g., AS9100, ITAR registration), or customer relationships will result directly from this project?
How will you isolate and measure the grant's contribution versus other business activities when reporting outcomes?
What data will you collect, how often, and who will be responsible for submitting performance reports to the agency?

sections.heading: Veteran-Owned and Small Business Status
sections.guidance: If the NOFO awards preference points or set-aside eligibility to veteran-owned businesses, this section substantiates that status. Document the ownership and control requirements precisely — federal definitions are strict and differ from state-level certifications.
promptsForApplicant: Is the veteran owner verified through the SBA Veteran Small Business Certification (VetCert) program, or only self-certified?
Does the veteran owner control day-to-day management and long-term decision-making of the firm as required under 13 CFR Part 128?
What documentation of veteran status (DD-214 or equivalent) is available to include in the application?
Does the NOFO for 2026-NIST-PMGP-01 explicitly provide a scoring preference or set-aside for veteran-owned businesses — and if not, is this section still relevant?

watchOuts: CRITICAL — The opportunity record (2026-NIST-PMGP-01) contains no description, no eligibility text, no funding range, no award count, and no close date. You MUST retrieve the actual Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO) from Grants.gov or NIST.gov before building a real application — every section above may need to be restructured once you see the actual submission format.

Years in operation risk: Many NIST and DOC manufacturing grant programs require a minimum operating history (often 2–3 years). At 1.5 years, you may be ineligible — verify the NOFO's minimum age-of-business requirement before investing time in this application.

For-profit entity treatment: For-profit firms are subject to different cost principles than nonprofits under federal grants. Confirm whether this opportunity awards grants or cooperative agreements to for-profits, and whether profit is an allowable cost.
SAM.gov registration: Your entity must have an active SAM.gov registration with a current Unique Entity Identifier (UEI) before submission — expirations or errors are a hard disqualifier.

Revenue and size standards: Confirm you meet the NAICS 332710 SBA small business size standard (currently 500 employees or a revenue threshold — check the current table). Also verify the NOFO does not impose a lower revenue ceiling that your $420K revenue either fails to meet (too small) or exceeds.
Cost-share trap: If the NOFO requires matching funds, confirm your source is non-federal. SBA loans, SBDC assistance, or other federal dollars cannot typically be used as match.

No agency identified: The funding agency field is blank. NIST PMGP is not a standard NIST acronym — confirm whether this is a NIST Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP) subgrant, a DOD program, an EDA award, or something else entirely, as the submission system, forms, and review criteria will differ substantially.

Ohio state registration: Confirm the business is in good standing with the Ohio Secretary of State and that your ZIP code (44114 — Cleveland) places you within any geographic targeting the grant may specify for distressed or priority manufacturing regions.

summary.intent: I'm a veteran-owned manufacturing startup in Ohio, 18 months old, 5 employees. What's available?

candidates.title: Meat and Poultry Processing Expansion Program - Phase 4
candidates.rationale: USDA Rural Business-Cooperative Service and co-op eligibility are relevant, but this program targets meat and poultry processing expansion, not grain storage or conveyance equipment modernization.

candidates.title: F24AS00298 Cooperative Agriculture
candidates.rationale: Title references cooperative agriculture under a USDA agency, but Fish and Wildlife Service cooperative agriculture programs focus on wildlife/habitat land management, not grain co-op equipment modernization.

candidates.title: Resident Instruction Grants for Institutions of Higher Education in Insular Areas Agriculture and Food Science Facilities and Equipment Program for Insular Areas

candidates.rationale: USDA agency and equipment theme are superficially relevant, but eligibility is restricted to institutions of higher education in insular areas, excluding an Iowa grain co-op.

candidates.title: Coordinating Agricultural Development & Innovation (CADI) Armenia Exchanges: U.S. Food Safety Regulations and Standards Training Program

candidates.title: Coordinating Agricultural Development & Innovation (CADI) Uzbekistan: U.S. Soy Marketing Promotion
candidates.rationale: USDA agency but targets U.S. soy marketing promotion in Uzbekistan, entirely unrelated to domestic grain co-op equipment modernization.

Recommended next steps: (1) Obtain the full opportunity announcement (NOFO/NOFA) for RD-RBS-26-04 and re-run assessment against verbatim eligibility criteria; (2) Register or renew SAM.gov registration immediately to obtain a UEI, as this is required for all federal grant applicants regardless of program; (3) If this is a USDA Rural Development / Rural Business-Cooperative Service opportunity (suggested by 'RD-RBS' prefix), co-ops in agricultural NAICS 111150 in Iowa may be common target beneficiaries, but this is not confirmed by any provided text.

steps.message: Drafting for RD-RBS-26-04 — best uncertain match in shortlist.

result.summary: A member-owned grain co-op in central Iowa with 18 years of operation and $4.8M in annual revenue seeks federal rural business development funding under RD-RBS-26-04 to modernize grain storage and conveyance infrastructure. The proposal angle centers on cooperative ownership structure serving rural agricultural producers, operational sustainability, and community economic impact in a USDA Rural Development priority region. Critical unknowns — funding range, close date, eligibility text, and full program description — must be resolved before any submission commitment is made.

sections.heading: Statement of Need
sections.guidance: Establish the rural agricultural problem being solved. Because no eligibility or program description was provided, frame need around USDA Rural Development Rural Business Service priorities: rural economic vitality, cooperative business models, and agricultural infrastructure gaps. Quantify the deficiency in current storage and conveyance capacity.
promptsForApplicant: What is the current grain storage capacity in bushels, and by what percentage does it fall short of member demand during peak harvest periods?
How old is the existing conveyance equipment, and what are the documented failure rates, downtime costs, or safety incidents in the last three years?
How many member-farmers depend on this co-op as their primary storage and marketing outlet, and what is the average farm size served?
What is the nearest competing storage facility, and what would members pay — in miles, time, or dollars — if this co-op could not serve them?
Are there documented market access losses (e.g., missed delivery windows, grade discounts) attributable to equipment limitations?

sections.heading: Project Approach
sections.guidance: Describe the specific modernization activities, equipment to be acquired or installed, timeline, and how implementation will be managed. Without a program description, align this section to USDA RBS norms: specific, time-bound activities, leveraged funds, and rural business growth outcomes.
promptsForApplicant: What specific storage or conveyance equipment will be purchased or installed — provide make, model, capacity specs, or at minimum functional descriptions?
What is the projected total project cost, and how will it be broken down between federal request, co-op equity, member assessments, or other leverage?
What is the proposed project timeline from award to full operational deployment, with key milestones?
Who will manage construction or installation — internal staff, a general contractor, or an equipment vendor — and have you obtained preliminary quotes?
How will operations continue for members during the transition or construction period?

sections.heading: Organizational Capability
sections.guidance: Demonstrate that the co-op has the governance, financial, and operational track record to manage federal funds and deliver the project. Cooperative structure is a program fit asset — highlight it explicitly. 18 years of operation and $4.8M revenue are credibility anchors.
promptsForApplicant: How is the co-op governed — board size, meeting frequency, officer roles — and who will have fiduciary oversight of the grant funds?
Has the co-op previously received federal or state grants, loans, or USDA program funds, and if so, what was the compliance outcome?
What are your current debt-to-asset ratio and working capital position, and can you provide three years of audited or reviewed financials?
Who on staff will serve as the day-to-day project manager, and what is their relevant experience with capital projects?
Do you have existing relationships with USDA Rural Development state office staff in Iowa, and have you had a pre-application conversation with them?

sections.heading: Budget Narrative
sections.guidance: Provide a detailed, line-item justification of all costs. Because the funding range is unknown, present a full project budget and clearly delineate which costs are being requested versus matched. USDA RBS programs typically require matching funds or leverage — document all sources.
promptsForApplicant: What is the total project cost, and can you provide a line-item breakdown covering equipment, installation labor, site preparation, permits, and contingency?
What percentage of total project cost are you planning to request from this grant, and where is the remaining funding coming from?
Are matching funds already committed, conditionally committed, or still being identified — and do you have commitment letters if applicable?
Are any cost items already incurred or contracted, which could make them ineligible as match or as grant-funded expenses?
What is the useful life of the equipment being purchased, and how does that relate to any required performance or compliance period?

sections.heading: Outcomes & Evaluation
sections.guidance: Define measurable outputs and outcomes tied to rural business and agricultural economic goals. USDA programs require performance metrics; anticipate reporting requirements even though the program description is absent.
promptsForApplicant: What specific, measurable outcomes will result from this project — e.g., additional bushels of storage capacity, reduction in throughput time, number of member-farmers directly benefiting?
Will the modernization enable the co-op to serve additional members or retain members who might otherwise leave — and by how many?
What jobs, if any, will be created or retained as a direct result of this project?
How will you track and document outcomes during and after the project, and who internally is responsible for that data collection?
What does success look like at 12 months and 36 months post-completion, and how will you report it to the funding agency?

watchOuts: CRITICAL: No program description, eligibility text, funding range, close date, or awarding agency was provided for RD-RBS-26-04. Do not invest significant proposal effort until you confirm this opportunity exists in Grants.gov or the USDA RD website, obtain the full NOFA, and verify the co-op meets stated eligibility criteria.

Co-op entity type is eligible under many USDA Rural Business Service programs, but some RBS opportunities restrict applicants to non-profits, municipalities, or tribal entities — confirm cooperative businesses are explicitly listed as eligible applicants in the actual NOFA.

Iowa ZIP 50010 (Ames area) must qualify as a rural area under USDA definition (typically a non-urbanized area or community under 50,000 population for RBS programs) — verify the project site meets the rural place-based eligibility test.

Annual revenue of $4.8M may trigger a size standard ceiling in some RBS programs. Confirm whether the program imposes a revenue or employee cap and whether cooperative member revenue is aggregated with the co-op entity's revenue for that calculation.

The co-op does not hold any socially disadvantaged, woman-owned, veteran-owned, or minority-owned status. If the program awards scoring preferences or set-asides for those designations, the co-op will compete without those point advantages — build strength in other scoring criteria.

If equipment has already been ordered or purchased prior to award, those costs are almost certainly ineligible. Do not proceed with procurement before award unless you have explicit written authorization from the program office.

Without a confirmed close date, there is no way to assess whether adequate time exists to obtain board authorization, gather financial documents, secure commitment letters for matching funds, and complete the application — confirm the deadline immediately.
